title: How an image become a Gen Z protest symbol | FT #shorts

transcript.text: This flag has been popping up at
protests around the world. It's the
Straw Hat Pirates Jolly Rodger. The flag
has appeared this year at Gen Zed le
demos, including in Indonesia, Nepal,
and at Mexico's recent protests, too.
The image comes from One Piece, one of
Japan's most successful, longestr
running manga series. At a time of
political discord and social distress,
young people have embraced it and the
story of Monkey Duffy, One Piece's
teenage hero, too. [music] In the
cartoon, Luffy sails the globe with his
crew of Straw Hat pirates, fighting
warlords, tyrants, and an all powerful
world government. The manga's storyline
has a strong anti- athoritarian streak.
The pirate flag was seen on the streets
as early as 2023 when pro Palestinian
protesters raised it in Yoga Carta,
Indonesia. The image then went viral.
[music] It was seen at other protests in
Indonesia, where anime has long been a
staple of youth culture, and in London,
too. But the symbol really took off
during violent protests in Nepal in
September. That's when the flag appeared
in news photos against the dramatic
backdrop of a burning government
building. The Straw Hat Pirates Jolly
Roger then spread on a global wave of
youth protests disseminated via
platforms like Discord and Tik Tok. It
was spotted, among other places, at a
pro Palestine protest outside the UN, in
France and in Slovakia, and in
Madagascar, where anti-government
protesters gave the pirate a colorful
local hat.
This isn't the first time young people
have minded a pop culture franchise to
make a political point. Jenzuters raised
the three-finger salute from the Hunger
Games as an emblem of protest in
Thailand in 2020 and in Myanmar after
the 2021 coup. As for the Straw Hat
Pirates, what started as a Japanese
cartoon is now a global rally and cry.
